original_abstract:
This article examines the institutional evolution and structural organization of the Kyiv Theological Academy, positioning it as a premier center for spiritual education, scholarly research, and cultural development within Ukraine and the broader Russian Empire. The study highlights the Academy’s role as a vital "personnel forge," producing highly qualified graduates who filled essential roles in both ecclesiastical and secular educational institutions, as well as throughout various levels of state and local administration.

The research contextualizes the Academy within the broader educational reforms of the Russian Empire, which established a hierarchical vertical of spiritual instruction. Under this system, theological academies served as specialized higher education hubs, each presiding over one of four distinct spiritual-educational districts: Kyiv, Kazan, Moscow, and St. Petersburg. Central to this institutional history is the transformative impact of the official Orthodox Theological Academy charters of 1814, 1869, 1884, and 1910. These regulatory frameworks dictated the Academy’s staffing protocols, student enrollment, faculty appointment procedures, and the criteria for conferring academic degrees and ranks.

Furthermore, the paper delineates the governance of the Academy through its collegiate bodies, specifically the Academic Conference and the internal and external (district) Boards, which held direct administrative authority. Finally, the study explores the leadership dynamics of the senior management—namely the rector and inspector—illustrating their operational roles and their vertical relationship with other governing entities within the imperial higher education system.
humanized_abstract:
The article explores the development of the institution and the structure of the Kyiv Theological Academy and examines its role as one of the most important institutions of spiritual education, scientific research and cultural development in Ukraine and the Russian Empire. The Academy was considered to be a major personnel forge, which continuously produced highly qualified specialists who occupied key educational and scientific state and local government positions in the church and state structures.

The Research paper presents the historical and educational context of the Academy within the system of educational reforms implemented in the Russian Empire, which formed the vertical of spiritual education, with Theological Academies serving as the Higher Education institutions for each of the four spiritual and educational districts established in Kyiv, Kazan, Moscow and St. Petersburg. Essential to understanding the development of the Academy is the significance of the official Orthod. The official Orthodox Theological Academy charters of 1814, 1869, 1884 and 1910 determined such issues as staffing, student numbers, as well as the requirements for appointing teachers and for granting degrees and academic ranks to students.

The paper also describes the government of the Academy through its collegiate organs: the Academic Conference, and the internal and external (district) Boards with administrative authority. Finally, the study turns to the leadership of the senior management group – that of the rector and the inspector – and illustrates their activities and their vertical relations with the higher bureaucratic authorities in the imperial system of higher education.
